paragraphs.text: After this God appeared to Abram in a vision and said: “Thy reward shall be exceeding great (Gen 15:1), for thy progress in righteousness and for the deliverance of the captives by thee.” “And Abram said: …what wilt thou give me? for I go childless… and the steward of my house is my heir… And he brought him forth abroad, and said: Look now toward heaven, and number the stars, if thou be able to number them: and he said: So shall thy seed be. And Abram believed… and it was counted to him for righteousness” (Gen 15:2–3, 5–6) — and indeed for great righteousness. Since he believed something hard to fulfill, which not every man could have believed, this was counted to him for righteousness. Having praised Abram for his faith, God “said to him: I am the God that brought thee out of Ur of the Chaldees, to give thee this land to inherit it. And he said: …whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it?” (Gen 15:7–8). Some say that for Abram’s doubting of the promise it was said to him afterward: “Know of a surety that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs” (Gen 15:13). But let those who assert this know with what faith Abram at the same time received the promise that his seed would be as numerous as the sand of the sea. If Abram believed what was far more important — namely, that from him alone and from one barren and aged Sarai there would come forth a seed numerous as the sand — then certainly he who did not doubt the greater did not doubt the lesser, namely that his seed would inherit the land. If he had doubted, why would God have said to him: “Take… a heifer of three years old, and a she goat of three years old, and a ram of three years old, and a turtledove, and a young pigeon” (Gen 15:9)? This was said to Abram by night, and by day he fulfilled it, and from morning to evening he stood, driving away the birds that came down upon his sacrifice. And only after fire came down in the evening upon his pleasing sacrifice did God appear to him and speak of the sojourning of his seed. If God had pronounced this on him as a punishment, his sacrifice would not have been accepted, and God would not on that day have made a covenant with him, would not on that day have promised that ten nations should be subjected to his seed, would not have said that he himself would go to his fathers in a good old age. All this God uttered to Abram on that very day, because “Abram believed… and it was counted to him for righteousness” (Gen 15:6). How then do they say that on this day, because of want of faith, a punishment was pronounced upon the seed of him who, for the faith manifested on that same day, was deemed worthy of great rewards?

So Abram believed something hard to fulfill: that out of Sarai’s aged (old, ancient) and almost dead womb would come a whole world of people. As for inheriting the land, he asked not whether it would be, but how it would be. He saw that the land of Canaan had kings and armies, that it was full of inhabitants — and now he hears that it would be given not to him but to his seed, and therefore he wished to know how all this would be brought about, even though not in his time, how his seed would enter that land and receive it as their inheritance. Will the kings, Abram thought, destroy one another, or will other nations come, destroy its inhabitants, and make the land empty for us? Or will my seed become mighty, come, destroy its inhabitants, and receive it as an inheritance? Or will the land swallow up its inhabitants for their deeds, or will the inhabitants themselves move to another land because of famine, drawn by some rumor or otherwise? So Abram, when he questioned God, did not doubt the truthfulness of the promise, but only wished to know by which of these ways his seed would receive the land in inheritance. But God knew what Abram desired, and along with what he asked, He revealed to him also what he had not asked. By the fact that Abram drove away the birds of prey from the sacrifice he had prepared, God showed him that his seed would suffer evil for their sins, but by the prayers of the righteous would be saved. In the image of the fiery furnace that came down from heaven, God gave him to understand that salvation would be sent from heaven when there would be among them no righteous men. The heifer of three years old, the she-goat of three years old, and the ram of three years old represented either that Abram’s descendants would be delivered from bondage through three generations, or that from their midst kings, priests, and prophets would come forth. The division of the animals by sex foretokened the division of Abram’s descendants into tribes; and the fact that the birds were not divided meant their mutual concord.

Having shown this to Abram, God said: “Thou shalt know of a surety (which is what thou wished to know) that thy seed shall be a stranger in a land that is not theirs.” Thy descendants shall not go there as captives, but shall be removed thither on the beasts of burden and the heifers they shall have. “And they shall serve them… four hundred years” (Gen 15:13). They would not have entreated to be brought out from there, if they had not been subjected to heavy bondage. “But that nation, whom they shall serve, will I judge: and afterward shall they come out hither with great substance. And thou shalt go to thy fathers… in a good old age. But in the fourth generation they shall come hither again.” And the added words, “for the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet full” (Gen 15:14–16), show that the measure of sins had not yet been filled, for which the Amorites by justice would have been subjected to final destruction by the sword.

“A deep sleep fell upon Abram” (Gen 15:12). As sleep fell upon Abimelech (Gen 20:3), so it fell also upon Abram, when God appeared to him, made a covenant with him, declared that ten nations would be in bondage to his seed, and that all the land, from the river of Egypt to the Euphrates, would be given as an inheritance to his seed.
